Subject: Standard Bank Account eStatement 2021-06-25
Body: Dear Customer Please find your electronic statement for June attached. To keep your financial information safe, please note the following: Information that only you will know is displayed in the eStatement verification block. This is done so you can be sure your statement is from Standard Bank. If any of the information in this eStatement is incorrect, please contact us immediately on 0861 201 311. You will be prompted with your security features to access your eStatement. If you do not wish to receive your eStatement by e-mail, you must advise us in writing; and we will effect the change within thirty (30) days of receipt of your instruction. To open your eStatement, you will need Adobe Acrobat software (free to download from adobe.com). Regards, Standard Bank For any queries please call us on 0861201311 Our lines are open from 8am to 9pm Monday to Friday and 8am to 4pm on weekends and public holidays. Copyright Standard Bank. Subject: FW: NZCSRSC08 - submission deadline extended !!
Body: Looks like the deadline has been extended, Apparently the registration is cheap and there are funds to subsidise travelling and possibly free student accommodation at the venue, Cheers Chris -----Original Message----- From: Amali Weerasinghe [mailto:ukd05@student.canterbury.ac.nz] Sent: Friday, 7 December 2007 4:05 p.m. To: Kloss, Guy; Messom, Chris Cc: ilmwynf57qjrtley+il@gmail.com Subject: NZCSRSC08 - submission deadline extended !! Hi Guy & Chris I'm sending the notice re the extension of the submission deadline. It would be great if you can circulate it across the dept. I've also attached a text attachment. Could you pls. let me know when this has been done ? Thanks Amali ----------------------------------------------------------------------- The 6th New Zealand Computer Science Research Student Conference =================================================== 14-17 April 2008, Christchurch SUBMISSION DEADLINE HAS BEEN EXTENDED TO 21 DECEMBER 2007 ------------------------- Submissions are now open for the 6th New Zealand Computer Science Research Student Conference. There is less than one month before the submission deadline (21 December 2007). Instructions for using the submission utility are listed at http://nzcsrsc08.canterbury.ac.nz/submissions.html PAPERS ----------- Submissions are now being accepted for: * Full papers. A full paper must not exceed 8 pages. If accepted, one student author will be required to present the paper at the conference. * Short papers. A short paper must not exceed 4 pages. If accepted, the student author(s) will be required to display a poster during the poster session at the conference. Papers must be written by a student author and reflect the student's own research. Papers can contain research in progress i.e. research does not need to be complete for a paper to be accepted. Papers may be co- authored as long as the primary author is the student. Papers also must comply with the instructions for Blind Review. Please consult the Call For Papers (http://nzcsrsc08.canterbury.ac.nz/papers.html) for more details. IMPORTANT DATES ----------------------------- * Submission deadline: 21 December 2007 * Review Period: 2 Jan 2008 - 1 Feb 2008 * Camera ready deadline: 7 Mar 2008 * Conference: 14-17 April 2008 * CS Unplugged Workshop: 18 April 2008 COSTS ----------- The student registration fee is kept at a low $40. This includes the conference sessions (including workshops), the industry event, social event, and food. For those living outside Canterbury, subsidies will be available for accommodation and travel. We are working on further sponsorship to allow for bigger subsidies. Keep an eye on the website closer to the date, for more details. Expenses should not be placed as an issue when deciding to submit a paper. Contact Details ------------------ Local contacts: Guy Kloss (r.xewwa@massey.ac.nz) & Chris Messom (k.p.twqqdt@massey.ac.nz) Queries regarding the call for papers or submissions: Jay Holland (jah130@student.canterbury..ac.nz) General contact and queries: Amali Weerasinghe (smuln.sustrlwlelm@pg.canterbury.ac.nz)

Subject: The Big Quit Becomes The Big Stay | Gates’ Grad Advice | A New Future Of Work Newsletter
Body: Forbes Newsletters Plus: 3 Steps To Avoid Letting Data Overwhelm Your Decision-Making ADVERTISEMENT If there was any workplace phrase that defined my first year writing this newsletter, it was the “Great Resignation.” Until last fall, my coverage on this beat was defined by what companies were doing to retain their job-hopping workers, who, post-pandemic, were switching employers at record pace—or leaving employment entirely. I wrote about how companies were rolling out sabbaticals to help retain workers, mandating required vacation time and trying to lure employees with extra time off at the start of their tenures. The Great Resignation was a big cultural moment in the world of work, even if no one could really decide what to call it.What a change a year brings. Starting last fall, layoffs began mounting in the tech sector, and a shadow over the economy loomed, as more and more companies across industries froze hiring, slowed pay increases and sent some of those lofty perks—like extra mental health days or “laundry benefits”—by the wayside. Now, the Great Resignation may be giving way to the Great Resistance—to leaving jobs, that is—according to new data from ADP. In a post on ADP’s web site, ADP Chief Economist Nela Richardson said that in the first quarter of the year, voluntary job departures fell 5% compared with the same time last year, while the number of people returning to the job market has risen. Pay increases for job switchers peaked in June 2022 at 16.4%, but have been decelerating, with April pay gains for job switchers down to the lowest rate of growth since November 2021. “There are some signs that the ‘Big Quit’ of 2022 will lead to the ‘Big Stay’ of 2023,” Richardson said in the post, which Forbes’ Jack Kelly wrote about here. All too many people searching for a job these days are doing so not because they opted to leave—but because they got laid off. If that’s you, we have advice on how to answer key interview questions, negotiate your salary if you get an offer and write a powerful cover letter. If you’re planning to stay put and wait out this downturn before looking again, here are tips on developing the presence needed to advance, asking for help at work and promoting your side hustle.Starting next week, Emmy Lucas will be anchoring this newsletter, with a focus on news about jobs, career strategy and advice for early- and mid-career professionals. On Friday, I’ll be switching gears to launch our new Future of Work newsletter, which will be aimed at chief human resources officers, talent executives and other senior leaders focused on the trends, technologies and ideas shaping where the workplace is headed next. It’s part of a series of C-Suite newsletters we’ll be adding in the coming months, and you can sign up to receive the first edition here.It’s been a great run leading this newsletter over the past year and a half, but as it goes with careers, I’m excited to launch something new. As always, thanks to Emmy Lucas for helping me curate this edition, and hope you’ll consider signing up for our new newsletter! Jena McGregor Senior Editor, Careers and Leadership Strategy Russell Westbrook Means Business The former NBA MVP isn’t waiting until retirement to start building an empire. text:
The Big Quit Becomes The Big Stay | Gates’ Grad Advice | A New Future Of Work Newsletter

Forbes Newsletters Plus: 3 Steps To Avoid Letting Data Overwhelm Your Decision-Making ADVERTISEMENT If there was any workplace phrase that defined my first year writing this newsletter, it was the “Great Resignation.” Until last fall, my coverage on this beat was defined by what companies were doing to retain their job-hopping workers, who, post-pandemic, were switching employers at record pace—or leaving employment entirely. I wrote about how companies were rolling out sabbaticals to help retain workers, mandating required vacation time and trying to lure employees with extra time off at the start of their tenures. The Great Resignation was a big cultural moment in the world of work, even if no one could really decide what to call it.What a change a year brings. Starting last fall, layoffs began mounting in the tech sector, and a shadow over the economy loomed, as more and more companies across industries froze hiring, slowed pay increases and sent some of those lofty perks—like extra mental health days or “laundry benefits”—by the wayside. Now, the Great Resignation may be giving way to the Great Resistance—to leaving jobs, that is—according to new data from ADP. In a post on ADP’s web site, ADP Chief Economist Nela Richardson said that in the first quarter of the year, voluntary job departures fell 5% compared with the same time last year, while the number of people returning to the job market has risen. Pay increases for job switchers peaked in June 2022 at 16.4%, but have been decelerating, with April pay gains for job switchers down to the lowest rate of growth since November 2021. “There are some signs that the ‘Big Quit’ of 2022 will lead to the ‘Big Stay’ of 2023,” Richardson said in the post, which Forbes’ Jack Kelly wrote about here. All too many people searching for a job these days are doing so not because they opted to leave—but because they got laid off. If that’s you, we have advice on how to answer key interview questions, negotiate your salary if you get an offer and write a powerful cover letter. If you’re planning to stay put and wait out this downturn before looking again, here are tips on developing the presence needed to advance, asking for help at work and promoting your side hustle.Starting next week, Emmy Lucas will be anchoring this newsletter, with a focus on news about jobs, career strategy and advice for early- and mid-career professionals. On Friday, I’ll be switching gears to launch our new Future of Work newsletter, which will be aimed at chief human resources officers, talent executives and other senior leaders focused on the trends, technologies and ideas shaping where the workplace is headed next. It’s part of a series of C-Suite newsletters we’ll be adding in the coming months, and you can sign up to receive the first edition here.It’s been a great run leading this newsletter over the past year and a half, but as it goes with careers, I’m excited to launch something new. As always, thanks to Emmy Lucas for helping me curate this edition, and hope you’ll consider signing up for our new newsletter! Subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Adventures with x64, VS7 and VS8 on Windows
Body: At 1:14 PM +0000 5/29/07, KristjÃ¡n Valur JÃ³nsson wrote: >> -----Original Message----- >> >> Microsoft's command line cannot cope with two pathnames that must be >> quoted, so if the command path itself must be quoted, then no argument >> to >> the command can be quoted. There are tricky hacks that can work around >> this mind-boggling stupidity, but life is simpler if Python itself >> doesn't >> use up the one quoted pathname. I don't know if Microsoft has had the >> good >> sense to fix this in Vista (which I probably will never use, since an >> alternative exists), but they didn't in XP. > >Do you have any references for this claim? >In my command line on XP sp2, this works just fine: > >C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft Visual Studio 8\\VC>"c:\\Program Files\\TextPad 4\\TextPad.exe" "c:\\tmp\\f a.txt" "c:\\tmp\\f b.txt" > >Both the program, and the two file names are quoted and textpad.exe opens >them both. I pounded my head against this issue when working on a .bat file a few years back, until I read the help for cmd and saw the quote logic (and switched to VBScript). It's still there, in "help cmd". I had once found references to the same issue for the run command in Microsoft's online help. Perhaps it is fixed in SP2. If so, just change it and don't worry about users with earlier versions of Windows. -- ____________________________________________________________________ TonyN.:' ' _______________________________________________ Python-Dev mailing list zvllln-eum@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/python-dev Unsubscribe: http://mail.python.org/mailman/options/python-dev/python-dev%40tangomu.com
